1:
The witnesses read the opening of the disruption on two levels — as sober constitutional history and as a study in inherited folly. The grammatical-historical commentators set the stage. Keil and Delitzsch step back to survey the whole tragedy: with Solomon dead, the ten tribes renounce the Davidic sovereignty, and the one strong nation is 'rent asunder' into two small and weakened kingdoms whose mutual hostility would sap the covenant people's spiritual power. Ellicott presses the legal point: Rehoboam succeeds 'without question' to the throne of Judah, but must be 'made king' by the rest of Israel, who claimed a right to require conditions — just as the elders had made a league with David (2 Samuel 5:3; compare 2:4). The venue is itself an omen: not Jerusalem but Shechem, chief city of Ephraim, 'of ancient dignity even from patriarchal times,' and the natural capital of any northern revolt.

The puritan Matthew Henry supplies the moral and providential frame. Solomon, wisest of men, left but one son to bear his name — 'and he a fool' — proof that neither wisdom nor grace runs in the blood (Hos. 4:10). Henry catches the people's real design: their pretence 'was to make him king, but the design was to unmake him,' and they cunningly fixed the ceremony away from the city of David. The place was ominous, he adds, for at Shechem Abimelech had once set up himself (Judg. 9), though it had also been famous for the solemn convention of the tribes (Josh. 24:1). Henry judges the coming complaint of grievous taxes 'groundless and unjust' — the people 'did but render to Solomon the things that were Solomon's' — and yet he sees the deeper hand: 'It was from the Lord,' who left Rehoboam to his own folly, hiding from his eyes the things that belonged to his peace.

The reformed Gill and the wesleyan Clarke fill in the man. Both observe that Rehoboam, for all his father's thousand wives and concubines, was probably Solomon's only son — 'a poor, unprincipled fool' — and Clarke draws the lesson that Solomon's excess in matrimonial affairs cost the house of David a wise heir. Gill traces the calculation: Rehoboam, knowing of the threat that the kingdom should be rent from him, hoped that by treating at Shechem he might prevent it, 'yet it proved the most impolitic thing he could do, and hastened the rupture.' The voices do not so much debate as layer one reading upon another: the constitutional historians explain what the people were entitled to ask, and the preachers explain why the asking turned to ruin — a kingdom thrown away, as Henry says, by wilfulness and folly, while God quietly worked his righteous purpose through it.
2:
This verse and the next form one tangled sentence, and the witnesses divide over a textual question that touches Jeroboam's whereabouts. The grammatical-historical commentators do the grammar. Keil and Delitzsch untangle the construction: the protasis 'when Jeroboam heard' does not reach its apodosis until 'they came' in the following verse, with several circumstantial clauses intervening — 'he was still in Egypt, whither he had fled from king Solomon.' They note that the parallel in 2 Chronicles 10:2 simplifies the whole by a slight change of one Hebrew word, turning 'Jeroboam dwelt in Egypt' into 'Jeroboam returned from Egypt.'

Here the real division appears. Ellicott judges the Chronicles-and-Septuagint reading 'obviously far more probable' — that Jeroboam, on hearing of Solomon's death, returned from Egypt to his own city and was 'sent for' thence — and thinks it 'might be read in the Hebrew by a slight alteration of the text.' Keil and Delitzsch are more cautious, unwilling to correct the received text of Kings merely because the Chronicler's phrasing runs smoother; they let the harder reading stand and explain it. The dispute is narrow but real: whether the verse pictures Jeroboam still abroad and only later summoned, or already homeward-bound and then invited to Shechem.

The narrative's own logic gives the setting its weight. Jeroboam is 'the son of Nebat,' the Ephraimite officer whom Solomon had raised over the labour of the house of Joseph and who, after Ahijah's prophecy that he should rule ten tribes, had fled the king's face into Egypt for refuge with Shishak. His flight is the fruit of an earlier word of God, and his return is timed exactly to the hour of Israel's discontent. Gill notes that the people 'sent and called him,' making the exile-turned-fugitive their spokesman before Rehoboam — the man who best knew, from his old post over the levy, the weight of Solomon's yoke. Whatever the precise route of his homecoming, the witnesses agree on the providential shape of the moment: the man marked out for the northern crown is on the scene at the very instant the tribes are ripe to give it. The textual crux over 'in Egypt' or 'from Egypt' leaves that shape untouched; it only affects the order of his movements, not the fact that Jeroboam stands ready, by God's arranging and the people's summons, to become the voice and then the king of the ten tribes.
3:
With Jeroboam summoned home, the tribes present their case, and the witnesses read the assembly as an act at once orderly and ominous. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch complete the long sentence: 'they sent and called him,' and then 'Jeroboam and all the congregation of Israel came, and spake unto Rehoboam.' Their petition, weighed in the next verse, is that the yoke Solomon laid on them be lightened — literally, 'make light away from the service of thy father,' reduce what he imposed — and on that condition 'we will serve thee.' Keil and Delitzsch are careful to hold the two sides of the ledger together: Solomon 'had undoubtedly demanded greater performances from the people than they had previously been accustomed to,' both to maintain the splendour of his court and to carry out his vast buildings; yet in return he had secured them 'the blessings of undisturbed peace throughout his whole reign,' and great wealth from the trade and tribute of subject nations, 'so that there cannot have been any well-grounded occasion for complaint.'

The reformed Gill fills in the human machinery. It was 'the people of Israel, some of the principal of them, especially of the tribe of Ephraim,' who sent messengers inviting Jeroboam to Shechem; and, following Kimchi, he allows that this summons may itself have been the reason for his return from Egypt. The scene, then, is not a spontaneous mob but a deputation of tribal heads with a designated speaker — the very form of a national negotiation.

The whole force of the verse lies in the phrase 'all the congregation of Israel.' This is not a riot but the covenant assembly, the same body that had acclaimed David and Solomon, now come to bargain with a third king. Its presence lends the coming demand a constitutional weight, and its choice of Jeroboam as spokesman quietly signals where its heart already lies. The tribes come 'willing to be ruled, but not to be ridden' — ready to serve if the burden is eased, but gathered in strength and with a leader at their head. The commentators sense the balance of the moment: a lawful request is being made through lawful channels, yet the makings of revolt are all in place, needing only a rough answer to ignite it. The verse thus poises the reader between two possible outcomes — a renewed covenant or a shattered one — and everything will turn on how the young king weighs the plea that Israel has come, in due order, to lay before him.
4:
The petition itself now stands before us — 'Thy father made our yoke grievous' — and the witnesses divide over whether the complaint was just. The grammatical-historical Ellicott is the great champion of the tribes' good faith. From the very tone of the narrative, and especially the king's total absence of resentment at the conditions, he infers 'that they were acting within their right.' The imposition of heavy taxation and forced labour 'was against old traditions, and even against the practice of Solomon's earlier years' (1 Kings 4:20; 9:20-22), so that to ask its removal 'was perfectly compatible with a loyal willingness to serve the new king.' He grants that the demand 'might naturally be suggested by Jeroboam, who, by his official position, knew well the severity of the burden' — but that does not make it a rebel's pretext.

The wesleyan Clarke reads the two grievances as distinct and both severe: 'excessively laborious service, and a heavy taxation.' He sketches a darker arc: at first Solomon employed no Israelite in drudgery, but 'afterwards, when he forsook the God of compassion, he seems to have used them as slaves, and to have revived the Egyptian bondage.' On this reading the yoke was real oppression, and the plea the cry of an ill-used people.

The reformed Gill takes the opposite side. He lists the taxes' honest purposes — Solomon's buildings, household, horses and chariots, officers, and magnificent court — and insists 'they had very little reason to complain, since this was for the honour and grandeur of their nation,' while they lived 'in peace, plenty, and safety all his days,' silver made as common as stones. He allows one qualification: perhaps the burden was increased in Solomon's latter days to support his many wives and their idolatrous worship, and the defence of the realm against Hadad and Rezon. But his sharpest point is the one the tribes fail to raise: 'what they find most reason to complain of, they take no notice of, even the idolatry he had set up among them.' There is the real division — Ellicott and Clarke hear a legitimate grievance, Gill hears a factious and misplaced one — and yet even Ellicott concedes the request depended on 'the seriousness of the grievances,' and Gill concedes the burden may have grown at the last. All agree that the people's silence about Solomon's apostasy, while they clamour about their taxes, exposes where their true treasure lay: they cared for their money, not their God.
5:
Rehoboam's request for three days' delay looks at first like prudence, and the witnesses agree it was in fact a blunder. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read it charitably as procedure: 'in order that the request of the tribes might be maturely weighed,' the king appoints a second hearing and 'in the meantime discussed the matter with the older counsellors, who had served his father' (v.6). On its face this is the deliberation a wise ruler owes a weighty petition.

The reformed Gill grants the surface but exposes the miscalculation. 'At first sight' the delay 'may seem a point of prudence, to take time for deliberation and counsel'; but 'in his case and circumstance it was very imprudent.' Rehoboam 'might easily see there was discontent among the people, and a faction forming against him,' and by taking time to himself he 'gave them time to take their measures, and hasten and ripen them for a revolt.' Worse, the very asking betrayed his mind: from so guarded a reply 'they might plainly perceive he was not inclined to give them satisfaction.' Gill's verdict is that the wise course was the opposite of caution — 'it would have been the most advisable in him to have promised them at once that he would make them easy.'

The lesson the witnesses draw is one of timing in leadership. A concession freely and swiftly given wins hearts; the same concession dragged out of a reluctant king wins nothing, for the delay itself is read as coldness. In a moment of popular excitement, hesitation is not neutral — it is provocation, giving the discontented both the leisure to organize and the evidence that their fears are justified. The three days that Rehoboam thought would let tempers cool instead let the fire draw. And there is a quiet irony the commentators feel: the king consults, which is right, but consults in a way that ensures he will be flattered rather than corrected, for he will weigh the sober word of the old men against the reckless word of his companions and choose the latter. The interval he opens for reflection becomes the interval in which the kingdom is lost. Even Keil and Delitzsch's neutral account of the procedure sets up the tragedy the following verses unfold: the machinery of good counsel is present, but the heart that would heed it is not, and so the pause meant for wisdom serves only to ripen folly.
6:
The old counsellors give their advice, and the witnesses weigh both the wisdom of the advisers and the worth of their counsel. The reformed Gill honours the men themselves. Rehoboam's consulting them 'was very prudently done,' for 'as these were men in years, and had been his father's counsellors, from whom no doubt they had learnt much wisdom, being often in cabinet council with him, they were capable of giving him the best advice.' Age, experience, and long attendance on the wisest of kings had fitted them to read the moment rightly.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch report the counsel and note a grammatical nicety: the old men answer, and 'the singular is used because one of them spoke in the name of the whole.' Their advice, weighed fully at the next verse, is disarmingly simple — 'If thou wilt be subservient to this people to-day, and servest them,... they will serve thee for ever.' Keil and Delitzsch then trace the sequel plainly: 'But Rehoboam forsook this advice, and asked the younger ministers who had grown up with him,' who 'advised him to overawe the people by harsh threats.'

The heart of the verse is the counsel's paradox, which the witnesses savour: the way to be served for ever is to serve now. The old men understand that sovereignty rests, in the end, on consent, and that a king who stoops once at the right moment binds his subjects to him more surely than any threat. It is the wisdom of men who have watched power at close range for a lifetime and know how fragile a new throne is. The tragedy the verse sets in motion is not that Rehoboam lacked good counsel but that he had it and rejected it. The oldest and most tested voices in the realm hand him the key to his kingdom, and he lays it down. Their advice cost him only a soft word 'this day, this critical day'; his refusal cost him ten tribes. The commentators do not divide here: they agree that the elders' counsel was sound, that it flowed from genuine political and moral wisdom, and that the king's fatal weakness lay not in failing to seek advice but in preferring the advice that fed his pride to the advice that would have saved his crown. What follows is the unfolding of that single, self-destructive choice.
7:
The elders' maxim — 'If thou wilt be a servant unto this people... they will be thy servants for ever' — draws out the chapter's finest disagreement, over whether their counsel was noble statesmanship or mere cunning. The wesleyan Clarke reads it as a constitutional ideal. 'This is a constitutional idea of a king: he is the servant, but not the slave of his people; every regal act of a just king is an act of service to the state.' The king is 'the fountain of law and justice,' the executor of the laws in his own name; 'properly speaking, a good and constitutional king is the servant of his people, and in being such he is their father and their king.' For Clarke the way to secure obedience 'is to hold the reins of empire with a steady and impartial hand,' so that the people 'see that the king lives for them, and not for himself.' He ends almost as a benediction: 'Happy are the people who have such a king; safe is the king who has such a people.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott hears something colder. Both policies, he says, 'show how corrupt and cynical the government of Israel had become.' The old men's advice 'has no largeness of policy or depth of wisdom'; it is 'the characteristic advice of experienced and crafty politicians,' who understood 'the dangerous vehemence of popular excitement, and the facility with which it may be satisfied by temporary concessions.' It is to give 'good words' and to be 'for the moment a servant to the people,' perhaps meaning to abolish some grievances 'but by no means of yielding up substantial power.' Whether it was even 'superficially prudent' would depend, he adds, on the seriousness of the grievances and the temper of the people.

There is the division: for Clarke the counsel embodies the truest theory of kingship, service as the ground of authority; for Ellicott it is a manager's trick, a calculated appeasement with no principle behind it. The reformed Gill stands nearer Clarke, taking the advice at face value as the good and gracious course. Yet the two readings are not finally irreconcilable, and the witnesses seem half-aware of it: the same words may be spoken from wisdom or from craft, and their soundness as policy does not settle the motive of those who gave them. What all agree is that the counsel, whatever its spring, pointed to the one road that could have held the kingdom — and that Rehoboam, preferring flattery to either wisdom or craft, took the other.
8:
The turning point of the tragedy is Rehoboam's refusal of the elders, and the witnesses probe the temper behind it. The reformed Gill lays bare the wounded pride: the king 'did not rightly relish' the old men's counsel, 'nor cordially receive it; it did not suit with his haughty temper, he could not brook it, to stoop to his people.' To speak soft words 'he thought a lessening of his dignity,' something 'that looked like courting their favour,' and so he 'determined not to take the advice given him by the old men, but to seek for other.' The verse, on Gill's reading, is a portrait of vanity choosing its own flatterers.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch report the substance and the men. Rehoboam 'forsook this advice, and asked the younger ministers who had grown up with him,' and 'they advised him to overawe the people by harsh threats.' They gloss the boast that follows — 'My little finger is stronger than my father's loins' — as 'a figurative expression in the sense of, I possess much greater might than my father,' and they identify the 'scorpions' of the threat as 'whips with barbed points like the point of a scorpion's sting,' citing the rabbis and Isidore for the cruel instrument.

The witnesses feel the pathos of the phrase 'the young men that were grown up with him.' Rehoboam turns from the counsellors who had stood before Solomon to the companions of his own leisure — men who shared his tastes, flattered his self-importance, and had never governed anything. The choice is not merely of a policy but of a world: he prefers those who tell him what his vanity wishes to hear over those who tell him what his throne requires. Here the two schools converge from different angles — Gill diagnosing the inward disease of pride, Keil and Delitzsch supplying the outward form the disease took in the brutal counsel of threats and scorpion-whips. Both see that the fatal step is taken in this verse, before a word has been spoken to the people. Rehoboam has already chosen; the rough answer of the coming days is only the choice made audible. And the commentators leave the reader with the sober lesson that the ruin of the young king began not with a battle or a betrayal but with the company he kept and the counsel he could not bear to hear.
9:
Rehoboam repeats the people's plea to his younger circle, and the witnesses read the question as the pivot on which the kingdom turns. The reformed Gill is brief here, cross-referring his own note above, but the force of the verse lies in the wording he and the others weigh: 'What counsel give ye that we may answer this people, who have spoken to me, saying, Make the yoke... lighter?' The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the narrative forward, quoting the king's question and then the young men's fatal reply — the boast of the thicker little finger and the threat to exchange whips for scorpions — as one continuous movement toward the rough answer of the third day.

What the commentators notice is the significance of the king's very framing. He has already told the people their yoke was heavy and their request reasonable; now he asks not whether to grant relief but how to word a refusal. The plea has been reduced, in his mouth, to a problem of management — 'that we may answer this people' — as though the tribes were an obstacle to be handled rather than subjects to be served. The royal 'we' is telling: he consults his companions as partners in power, having dismissed as beneath him the fathers who governed with Solomon.

The repetition of Israel's own words — 'make the yoke which thy father put upon us lighter' — keeps the justice of the demand before the reader's eye even as the king prepares to trample it. The tribes asked only for lightening, not liberty; they offered service in exchange. That so modest and lawful a request should be met, in the king's deliberations, as a thing to be crushed rather than weighed is the measure of how far pride has already carried him. The verse is quiet, almost procedural, but the witnesses sense its gravity: this is the last moment at which the answer is still open, the hinge between a kingdom held and a kingdom halved. Rehoboam poses his question to exactly the wrong men, and the phrasing of it shows that his mind is made up before they speak. The reasonable petition of a whole people has become, in the counsels of the young king, merely the occasion for a display of strength — and the commentators let the reader feel the weight of what is about to be thrown away for the sake of a boast.
10:
The young men's counsel is given, and the witnesses read it as the voice of a spoilt and doomed generation. The wesleyan Clarke pauses first on who these companions were. It was a custom of many nations 'to educate with the heir to the throne young noblemen of nearly the same age,' which, he notes with Calmet, served two ends — it spurred the prince to emulation, and it let him learn the disposition of those who would one day be his highest officers. But the arrangement bred the disaster here: 'the old counsellors Rehoboam did not know; with the young nobility he had been familiar.' Their boast Clarke renders as a proverb — 'My little finger is thicker than my father's thigh' — meaning, 'as much as the thigh surpasses the little finger in thickness, so much does my power exceed that of my father, and the use I shall make of it, to employ and tax you, shall be in proportion.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott delivers the verdict of history. The young men are 'the spoilt children of a magnificent and luxurious despotism, of which alone they had experience,' and their advice is 'the language of the arrogant self-confidence, which mistakes obstinacy for vigour, and, blind to all signs of the times, supposes that what once was possible... must last for ever.' It is 'needlessly and absurdly offensive'; yet, he adds with a glance at his own nation's Stuart kings, it is 'a not unfrequent policy in revolutionary times,' holding that 'to yield in one point is to endanger the whole fabric of sovereign power,' and 'trusting to cow by threats the classes long subject to despotic oppression.' Such a policy, he warns, 'can succeed only when the popular disaffection is superficial.'

The reformed Gill keeps the moral edge, noting that the young men were 'putting words into his mouth' — words that owned the charge against Solomon and multiplied it. Between them the witnesses draw a single portrait: counsellors who confuse harshness with strength and have never learned that authority long strained will snap. Clarke supplies the sociology of how such men came to surround the throne; Ellicott supplies the political law they violated; Gill supplies the impropriety of their very phrasing. All agree that the advice is not merely cruel but stupid — a swaggering misreading of a people already at the edge of revolt — and that its coarse, boastful image, the little finger against the loins, is the perfect emblem of pride marching straight into ruin.
11:
The threat reaches its climax — 'my father chastised you with whips, but I will chastise you with scorpions' — and the witnesses concentrate on the cruel image and the reckless words that carry it. The reformed Gill fastens on the first clause, 'my father did lade you with a heavy yoke.' To put such words into the king's mouth 'was very unbecoming, if true,' for it owned the accusation against Solomon that a wiser son would have refuted; Gill allows only that it may be reported 'according to the sense of the people' rather than as Rehoboam's own admission. Either way the boast concedes the very charge it means to crush.

The wesleyan Clarke draws out the menace. 'Should you rebel, or become disaffected, my father's whip shall be a scorpion in my hand. His was chastisement, mine shall be punishment.' Following Isidore and Calmet, he explains the scorpion as 'a sort of severe whip, the lashes of which were armed with iron points, that sunk into and tore the flesh,' and notes too that among the Romans the scorpion was a military engine for shooting poisoned arrows, 'likened to the scorpion's sting, and the wound it inflicted.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott agrees on the instrument: the scorpion is 'probably, like the Roman flagellum, a whip, the lash of which is loaded with weights and sharp points.'

The witnesses do not dispute the meaning; they let its horror speak. A people who had asked only that their burden be lightened are told it will be doubled, and that any resistance will be answered not with the whip but with the barbed and tearing scorpion. It is the language of a tyrant who imagines that fear can do the work of loyalty. And the commentators feel the folly beneath the ferocity: the threat is uttered not to a cowed and helpless population but to a proud, wealthy, and half-rebellious one that has a leader ready at its head. To answer a modest petition with a promise of torture is to invite exactly the revolt it means to forestall. The scorpion image, which the expositors trace so carefully through rabbi and Roman and church father, becomes in their hands the very symbol of Rehoboam's misgovernment — power that has forgotten it rests on consent, and that mistakes the capacity to wound for the right to rule. The words are scarcely spoken before, in the next verses, they recoil upon the head that framed them.
12:
The three days expire and the parties reassemble, and the witnesses read this quiet verse as the moment the trap closes. The reformed Gill catches its one telling detail: 'Jeroboam came to him at the head of the people, being the head of the faction, and designed for their king.' The man who had come home from Egypt as a spokesman now stands plainly as a leader; the crowd behind him is no longer a petitioning assembly only but a party with its own candidate for the throne, waiting to see what the king will say.

The verse gains its weight from what the interval has done. The pause Rehoboam opened for deliberation (v.5) has ripened the discontent it was meant to soothe. 'Jeroboam and all the people came to Rehoboam the third day, as the king had appointed' — the very punctuality is ominous. They return exactly when summoned, in force and in order, because they have used the days well: to take their measures, to fix their resolve, and to gather behind the one man best fitted to head them. What the king let slip in delay, they have gathered up in preparation.

There is a grim symmetry the commentators feel between the two comings. Israel had first come with a modest plea and a conditional promise of service; now they come again, the same 'all the people,' but with a different spirit, for the king's guarded reply and the three days' silence have taught them what to expect. The stage is fully set: on one side a young king armed with a boast, on the other a whole people armed with a grievance and a leader. Gill's single phrase — 'the head of the faction, and designed for their king' — tells the reader that the revolt is, in effect, already organized, needing only the spark the next verse will supply. The verse also marks the last appearance of a united Israel standing before the house of David to be answered; when they leave this assembly they will leave as two nations. So the commentators let the plainness of the report carry its own tension. Nothing yet has been decided in words, but everything has been decided in fact: the delay has done its work, Jeroboam has taken his place at the head, and the appointed third day has arrived on which the kingdom will be won or lost by a sentence.
13:
The king gives his answer, and the witnesses mark the fatal word: 'the king answered the people roughly.' The reformed Gill hears in it the whole disaster in miniature — Rehoboam spoke 'in a blustering manner, gave them hard words and severe menaces, being worked up to such a spirit by his young counsellors.' The verse names both the manner and its source: the harshness is the king's, but the spirit behind it has been kindled by the reckless companions whose advice he chose over the elders' in the earlier verses.

The single adverb 'roughly' carries more than tone; it carries the ruin of a kingdom. To a people who had come with a courteous petition and an offer of loyal service, the king returns not merely a refusal but a rebuff — brutality where they had asked for relief. The verse deliberately couples the two clauses: 'the king answered the people roughly, and forsook the old men's counsel.' The roughness is precisely the forsaking made visible. Every soft word the aged counsellors had urged, every stooping this-day service that would have bound the tribes to him for ever, is swept aside in one blustering sentence.

The commentators feel the pathos of a man delivering, with evident relish, the very speech that will unmake him. Rehoboam mistakes vehemence for majesty and bluster for strength; he imagines that a hard face will overawe a proud people. Instead the roughness confirms their worst fears and hands them their pretext. The tragedy is not that the king lacked the words to hold his kingdom — the old men had given them to him, ready-made — but that he could not bring himself to speak them, and chose instead the language that flattered his own sense of power. Gill's brief note, that he was 'worked up to such a spirit by his young counsellors,' locates the responsibility exactly: the folly is Rehoboam's, freely chosen, yet fed by the company he preferred. The verse thus stands as the crisis of the whole narrative, the instant at which the decision made privately in his counsels becomes the public act that rends Israel. One rough answer, and the assembly that came to make him king over all Israel will depart to make another king in his place. The witnesses let the starkness of the word 'roughly' do its work, a single stroke of the pen recording the moment a throne was thrown away.
14:
The content of the rough answer is now given, and the witnesses note that Rehoboam adopted not merely the policy of the young men but their very phrases. The reformed Gill puts it in a single stroke: he 'spake to them after the counsel of the young men, and in the very language in which they gave it.' The king had asked his companions how to answer the people (v.9); now he repeats their words as his own — the boast of the little finger thicker than his father's loins, the vow to add to the yoke, the threat to exchange whips for scorpions.

The witnesses feel the significance of that borrowing. It is one thing to be counselled to severity; it is another to take the counsel down to its coarsest word and utter it before a whole nation as a royal decree. Rehoboam does not soften the young men's swagger or temper their menace; he delivers it wholesale, owning in his own mouth the accusation against his father's government and pledging to make the burden heavier still. The verse shows a king who has surrendered his judgment entirely to his flatterers, speaking their lines as though they were his convictions.

The deliberate echo of the earlier verses — 'My father made your yoke heavy, and I will add to your yoke' — lets the reader hear the folly twice over, first as advice and now as policy. What was reckless when proposed becomes catastrophic when proclaimed, for these are no longer words in a private council but the answer of the crown to the covenant assembly of Israel. The people had offered service for relief; they are promised oppression for their loyalty. And the commentators sense the self-destruction folded into the very form of the speech. By repeating the young men's phrases exactly, Rehoboam betrays that he has nothing of his own to say — no wisdom, no measure, no sense of the people before him — but only the borrowed bravado of men as untried as himself. Gill's terse observation, that the king spoke 'in the very language in which they gave it,' is a quiet indictment: the ruler of Israel has become the mouthpiece of his most foolish advisers. The next verse will name the deeper hand at work in it all; but at the level of human choice, this verse records a king freely speaking his kingdom away, syllable by borrowed syllable.
15:
Beneath the human folly the narrator now names a divine hand — 'the cause was from the Lord' — and the witnesses gather around the great mystery of providence and freedom. The grammatical-historical Ellicott states it most fully. Scripture's very idea, 'referring all things to God, necessarily brings us continually face to face with the great mystery of life — the reconcilement of God's all-foreseeing and all-ordaining Providence with the freedom, and, in consequence, with the folly and sin of man.' As a rule, he says, Holy Writ 'simply recognises both powers as real, without any attempt to harmonise them together,' referring all to God's will while 'invariably' implying human responsibility: 'Rehoboam's folly and arrogance worked out the ordained judgment of God; but they were folly and arrogance still.'

The reformed voices press the sovereignty. Gill reads it plainly: the king 'hearkened not unto the people' because God so ordered, that the word spoken by Ahijah might stand. Matthew Henry, drawing near, sees God leaving 'Rehoboam to his own folly,' hiding from his eyes 'the things which belonged to his peace, that the kingdom might be rent from him,' and concludes that God 'serves his own wise and righteous purposes by the imprudences and iniquities of men, and snares sinners in the work of their own hands.'

The wesleyan Clarke offers the verse's one distinctive turn, a philological outlier. He is unwilling to let 'the cause was from the Lord' be read as though God authored Rehoboam's cruelty. The Hebrew word, he argues, is not the folly but the sibbah — 'the Revolution' or 'turning away' — and it was this that was from the Lord: 'God stirred up the people to revolt from a man who had neither skill nor humanity to govern them.' He notes that none of the ancient translations render it as the common version does, and even likens it to England's revolution of 1688. On his reading the thing ordained is the just removal of an unfit king, not the sin by which it came about.

The witnesses do not finally divide, for Clarke's careful distinction is only a sharper form of Ellicott's mystery: all agree that God overruled without authoring the evil, that the folly was truly Rehoboam's and the judgment truly God's. The verse becomes their shared confession that the Lord accomplishes his righteous ends through, and never in spite of, the free and guilty choices of men.
16:
The rupture breaks open, and the witnesses read Israel's war-cry — 'What portion have we in David?... To your tents, O Israel!' — as both a just recoil and a grievous sin. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the depth beneath the words. The harsh answer gave the discontented 'an apparently just occasion,' but these were the very words with which Sheba had once preached rebellion (2 Samuel 20:1), and they voice 'the deep-rooted aversion' to the Davidic monarchy long cherished by these tribes and 'only suppressed for the time by David and Solomon.' The real foundation of the secession, they insist, was 'the ancient jealousy of the tribes,' not merely Solomon's exactions. Ellicott hears the sarcasm in the last line — 'now see to thine own house, David,' which the Septuagint renders 'feed thine own house' — 'Be a king, but only in thine own house!'

The puritan Matthew Henry weighs the deed morally with characteristic balance. The people 'were bold and resolute in their revolt,' concluding rightly that a government so haughty in its beginning would be intolerable in its progress; yet they 'speak here very unbecomingly of David, that great benefactor of their nation,' calling him coldly 'the son of Jesse' — 'how soon are good men, and their good services to the public, forgotten!' Their haste, too, 'was much to be blamed,' for with patience and prudent management 'they might have settled the original contract with Rehoboam.' Henry holds both truths at once: their zeal for liberty 'well became that free people' — 'they are willing to be ruled, but not to be ridden' (Jer. 2:14) — but to rebel against the seed of David whom God had established, and set up a rival king, 'was a great sin' (2 Chron. 13:5-8; Hos. 8:4, 'they have set up kings, but not by me').

The reformed Gill and the wesleyan Clarke record the outcome soberly. 'Israel departed unto their tents' — the ten tribes withdrew their allegiance, Clarke notes, 'only Judah and Benjamin, frequently reckoned one tribe, remaining with him.' The witnesses converge on a double verdict that they refuse to simplify: the revolt was a righteous judgment on an arrogant king and, at the same time, a rebellion against God's anointed house. Both things are true, and the commentators will not surrender either — the same act that vindicates a free people's refusal to be trampled also indicts them for despising the covenant and the great king whose name they now speak with contempt.
17:
A quiet exception is registered — 'the children of Israel which dwelt in the cities of Judah, over these Rehoboam reigned' — and the witnesses unfold its double meaning. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify these people concretely: they are 'members of the ten tribes who had settled in Judah in the course of ages,' and the Simeonites especially, whose territory lay within Judah's own borders and who were therefore bound to remain in the southern kingdom (Joshua 19:1). The verse, they insist, cannot mean that 'the tribe of Judah declared in favour of Rehoboam,' as if Judah were the subject; rather it marks the scattered Israelites in Judah's cities who stayed under the Davidic crown.

Ellicott finds the phrase 'doubly significant.' Historically, Judah 'had its semi-dependent tribes' — Simeon absorbed into it, part of Dan, and Benjamin knit to it by the position of Jerusalem — so that 'the territory of the southern kingdom would be really the Judaea of later times.' And after Jeroboam's idolatry, 2 Chronicles 11:13-16 tells how priests, Levites, and other faithful Israelites made their way into Judah's cities. But there is a further weight in the very words 'children of Israel': though the northern kingdom would seize the proud title of Israel, the phrase 'is perhaps intended to remind the reader that in Judah also dwelt children of Israel — true descendants of the Prince of God, and inheritors of the promise.'

The reformed Gill offers the same reading with a pastoral turn, taking these as Israelites 'who had before dwelt, or now upon this removed, for the sake of worship,' to live in Judah's territory — people who valued the true sanctuary above their tribe. The witnesses do not divide; they build one point together. The disruption was political and, soon, religious, but it did not run cleanly along tribal lines. Grace kept a remnant of the ten tribes loyal to David's house and to the temple, and the narrator is careful to record them. The verse guards against a false tidiness: 'Israel' has departed to its tents, yet true Israelites remain in Judah, so that the covenant promise to David is not confined to one tribe but gathers to itself all who would keep faith. Ellicott's closing thought gives the verse its enduring note — the name 'Israel' belongs, in the end, not to the larger kingdom but to the faithful.
18:
Rehoboam's next move turns disaffection into open rebellion, and the witnesses read the sending of Adoram as folly compounded. The reformed Gill sees at once how disastrous the choice of envoy was. Adoram 'was over the tribute'; the heavy taxes 'were their complaint, and a tax gatherer, and especially one that was at the head of the tribute, must be of all men the most disagreeable to them.' Whether the king sent him to collect the tribute in a show of authority or, as Josephus thought, to reopen negotiations, 'it was too late, and he employed a very improper person' — 'another instance of the folly and false steps of Rehoboam.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the king's intention more sympathetically but the effect the same. Rehoboam 'was no doubt serious in his wish to meet the demands of the people,' and entrusted the negotiation to Adoram precisely because the complaint had been of the tribute-burden; 'but the very fact that he sent this man only increased the bitterness of feeling, so that they stoned him to death,' and the king himself had to 'summon up all his strength' to flee to his chariot and escape a like fate. Ellicott agrees: too late Rehoboam desired to deal through Adoram with the grievance of forced labour, 'but the sight of the man, who had been the taskmaster of their oppression, naturally stirred the multitude to a fresh burst of fury.' He and Keil and Delitzsch note that this Adoram (or Adoniram) had held the same office under David and Solomon (2 Samuel 20:24; 1 Kings 4:6), so that the name and office were probably hereditary — the face of the levy across a generation.

The wesleyan Clarke sums the whole misjudgment: to send the superintendent of the taxes 'when the public mind was in such a state of fermentation was another proof of Rehoboam's folly and incapacity to govern.' The witnesses are united. Even granting the king a genuine wish to conciliate, they see him blundering again at the decisive instant — dispatching, of all men, the very symbol of the burden the people had begged to be eased. The result is the first blood of the schism: Adoram stoned, and the king in undignified flight. Rehoboam, Gill says, 'was now as unhappy in the choice of his ambassador as before of his counsellors.' The revolt is now irreversible, sealed not by a word but by a killing, and the young king who had boasted of scorpions is running for his life to Jerusalem.
19:
The historian closes the account of the breach with a solemn formula — 'so Israel rebelled against the house of David unto this day' — and the witnesses weigh both its moral word and its historical one. The reformed Gill fixes on the term 'rebelled.' The revolt 'is called a rebellion; for though it was according to the purpose and will of God, yet the people had no command or order from God for it, and was done without consulting him, and was a rejection of their lawful prince.' Here Gill holds together the two truths the chapter keeps pressing: the secession fulfilled God's decree, and yet it remained a genuine sin, undertaken by the people's own will against the king God had set over them. Divine ordination did not excuse human rebellion.

The grammatical-historical voices attend to the phrase 'unto this day.' Keil and Delitzsch simply render the finality — 'thus Israel fell away from the house of David unto this day' — and read the following verses as completing the secession, all Israel calling Jeroboam to the congregation and making him king, 'so that the tribe of Judah alone adhered to the house of David.' Ellicott draws the critical inference from the formula: 'the phrase argues the incorporation into the narrative of an older document.' The words 'unto this day' were true for the writer of that source — Gill notes the rupture 'continued until their captivity by the king of Assyria' — and their preservation shows the historian faithfully embedding an earlier record.

Between them the witnesses give the verse its full resonance. It is at once a verdict and a date-stamp: a verdict that names the deed rebellion for all its providential necessity, and a date-stamp that fixes the division as a settled, enduring fact of Israel's life, not a passing tumult. The breach that opened at Shechem would not close; the ten tribes would remain sundered from Judah through the whole history that follows, down to their scattering by Assyria. Gill's careful balance is the heart of the matter — that a thing may be truly God's purpose and truly man's crime, the one not cancelling the other. The people threw off their lawful king by their own choice and stand answerable for it, even as the hand of God is seen accomplishing through their sin the word he had spoken by his prophet. The formula 'unto this day' seals it: what was done in a day of passion became the long, grievous condition of a divided people.
20:
The secession is formally completed as Israel crowns Jeroboam, and the witnesses probe both the act and its legitimacy. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the constitutional moment: 'all Israel called Jeroboam to the assembly of the congregation and made him king over all Israel, so that the tribe of Judah alone adhered to the house of David,' and they note that the verse opens as the earlier verse did, to signal that it closes the account there begun. Ellicott adds a shrewd political reading: the Shechem assembly had 'broke up in disorder,' and it would be 'quite in harmony with Jeroboam's astuteness' if, having set the revolution on foot, he 'stood aloof from leadership' until the duly summoned congregation sent for him and offered him the crown — the pose of a man drafted rather than grasping. The title 'king over all Israel,' he observes, asserts a claim by the ten tribes 'to be the true Israel,' perhaps leaning on the prophetic blessing of Jeroboam and professing to rise against the idolatry of Solomon's house.

The wesleyan Clarke raises the question of right most sharply. What is called 'Israel' here 'was ten-twelfths of the whole nation; and had they a right to call another person to the throne? They had not — they had neither legal nor constitutional right. Jeroboam was not of the blood royal; he had no affinity to the kingdom.' Only one thing could justify the act: 'the just judgment of God.' By it 'God thus punished a disobedient and gainsaying people, and especially Solomon's family, whose sins against the Lord were of no ordinary magnitude.'

The reformed Gill fills in the human detail, noting that while the chief men had already known of Jeroboam's return and headed the address to Rehoboam, 'the people in common had not' — so the news of his coming again now brought the whole body of the tribes to make him king. Between the witnesses a single picture forms with an unresolved tension held honestly within it: constitutionally the coronation was illegitimate, a people usurping a right that was not theirs and setting up a king outside the promised line; and yet the whole was the execution of a divine sentence on the house of David and on a faithless nation. Ellicott's note on Jeroboam's astute self-effacement and Clarke's insistence on the illegality together expose the mixture of human calculation and human presumption through which God's righteous judgment was, all the same, being carried out.
21:
Rehoboam reaches Jerusalem and prepares for war, and the witnesses read his muster as brave, natural, and yet against God. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch report the array: back in his capital 'he was still desirous of bringing back the seceders by force of arms, and raised for that purpose an army of 180,000 men' out of Judah, Benjamin, and 'the rest of the people' — the Israelites dwelling in Judah's cities — a number they judge 'does not appear too large' when set beside the census of 2 Samuel 24:9. The force is real and formidable, gathered to fight 'against the house of Israel, to bring the kingdom again to Rehoboam.'

The reformed Gill notes only the plain geography — Jerusalem lay 'forty miles from Shechem' — but the distance quietly underscores the king's changed fortunes: the man who had come north to be crowned over all Israel now retreats south to raise soldiers against most of it. Keil and Delitzsch, looking ahead to Shemaiah's oracle, mark the theological hinge: the prophet will forbid the war 'for this thing was from the Lord,' the phrase pointing, as one interpreter puts it, to Rehoboam's being 'deprived of the sovereignty over ten tribes,' not to the tribes' rebellion — the same providential 'thing' already named earlier in the chapter.

The witnesses feel the pathos and the peril of the moment together. Humanly, the muster is understandable and even creditable: a king does not surrender ten tribes of his inheritance without a struggle, and Judah and Benjamin rally to him loyally, a great host of chosen warriors ready to reclaim his right. Yet the whole enterprise is set squarely against the declared purpose of God, who has himself rent the kingdom away. Rehoboam's courage, which had failed him at Shechem where a soft word would have saved all, now returns to him in Jerusalem where a sword can only make matters worse. The commentators leave the army standing armed and eager on the page precisely so that the next verses may show a greater power halting it. The 180,000 chosen men are drawn up for a battle that God will not permit, and the verse hangs on that suspense — a kingdom's might assembled to undo what heaven has done, waiting for the word of a prophet to tell it to go home. The strength is genuine; the cause is hopeless, because it contends not merely with Jeroboam but with the Lord.
22:
The prophetic word arrives to arrest the war, and the witnesses attend to the messenger and his commission. The reformed Gill identifies him simply: Shemaiah is 'a prophet in those days,' and the word that came to him is, as the Targum has it, 'the word of prophecy' — a genuine oracle of the Lord breaking in upon the king's counsels of war. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the message in full, addressed 'unto Rehoboam... and unto all the house of Judah and Benjamin, and to the remnant of the people,' with the command not to go up against their brethren, 'for this thing is from me.' They then read the sequel: 'they hearkened to the word of the Lord, and returned to depart,' the muster disbanded at a prophet's word.

Ellicott draws out what makes Shemaiah's intervention so striking. From the notices in 2 Chronicles 12:5-8 it appears that, while Ahijah 'belonged to Shiloh in Ephraim' — the prophet who had foretold Jeroboam's rise — Shemaiah 'was rather attached to Judah'; hence 'his interference to protect the new kingdom was the more striking and unexpected.' A prophet of Judah forbids Judah's king to reclaim the ten tribes and so shields the very secession that wounds his own nation. In this, Ellicott sees 'a fresh indication of the great opportunity given to that kingdom to maintain itself, under the blessing of God and in devotion to His service.' The phrase 'your brethren, the children of Israel,' he adds, 'marks this with much emphasis.'

The witnesses converge on the wonder of it. The war that no human calculation could have stopped — a great army mustered, a king resolved, a cause that seemed both just and feasible — is halted by a single unarmed man carrying a word. That the word came through a prophet of Judah, against Judah's own interest, underscores that it was truly God's and not a partisan's. The oracle both forbids the bloodshed and interprets the whole disaster: 'this thing is from me.' The division of the kingdom is not an accident to be reversed by force but a judgment to be received in submission. The commentators note, too, the emphasis on 'brethren': Israel and Judah remain kin even in their sundering, and God will not have brother slay brother to undo what he has ordained. Shemaiah, otherwise unnamed in the record, appears for this one decisive moment to bend a king and an army to the will of heaven.
23:
The oracle's address is spelled out, and the witnesses find its very wording charged with meaning. The reformed Gill notes the striking title given the king: the word is to be spoken 'unto Rehoboam the son of Solomon, king of Judah.' He observes that Rehoboam has been called king before in the chapter, 'but of what tribe or tribes is not expressly said'; only now, and by the Lord himself, is his realm named — 'and he is only acknowledged king of Judah by the Lord himself.' Heaven ratifies the reduction. The kingdom that Rehoboam still hoped to reclaim over all Israel is, in God's own address to him, defined and limited: he is king of Judah, and of Judah only.

The breadth of the address is as telling as its title. The word comes 'to all the house of Judah and Benjamin, and to the remnant of the people' — that is, to the whole body that had rallied to Rehoboam, including the scattered Israelites who had cast in their lot with the south. God does not speak to the king alone but to the assembled nation-in-arms, for it is the whole host that must be turned from the battle. The message is not a private counsel but a public decree, laid upon everyone who had taken up the sword.

The commentators feel the quiet finality of the phrase 'king of Judah.' Throughout the chapter the language has hovered — 'all Israel,' 'the congregation,' 'the house of David' — but here, in the mouth of the Lord, the new map of the covenant people is drawn with a single stroke. Rehoboam's dominion is fixed at its true and diminished bounds; the ten tribes are not his to recover. That the divine word itself frames him thus is the strongest possible confirmation of what Shemaiah is about to forbid: the war cannot succeed, because God has already accepted and named the division. The verse, brief as it is, functions as the seal of heaven upon the events at Shechem. What the people did in rebellion and Jeroboam gained by astuteness, God now incorporates into his own settled order, addressing Rehoboam by the only title that henceforth belongs to him. The remnant who followed him are included in the address precisely so that they too may learn to accept the boundary the Lord has set, and go home. Everything in the verse points toward the command of submission that immediately follows.
24:
The command itself is given and obeyed — 'Ye shall not go up, nor fight against your brethren... for this thing is from me' — and the witnesses dwell on the remarkable submission it drew. The reformed Gill, prefacing the oracle with the prophets' customary 'Thus saith the Lord,' lets the divine word stand in its plain authority: the separation of the ten tribes is God's doing, and the armed nation must not undo by force what heaven has ordained.

The wesleyan Clarke draws out the weight of the obedience. 'This thing is from me — that is, the separation of the ten tribes from the house of David.' And the response is astonishing: 'They returned to depart. This was great deference, both in Rehoboam and his officers, to relinquish, at the command of the prophet, a war which they thought they had good grounds to undertake.' Here was an army of 180,000 chosen men, a king with a plausible claim and a real prospect of success, halted and sent home by a single word. Clarke marks the rareness of it, and adds a reflection on the common people who made up the host: 'The remnant of the people heard the Divine command gratefully, for the mass of mankind are averse from war. No nations would ever rise up against each other, were they not instigated to it or compelled by the rulers.'

The witnesses join the two notes — the sovereignty of the word and the grace of the obedience. It would have been the easiest thing in the world for a proud young king, freshly humiliated and now backed by a great army, to press on; the losses of Shechem cried out to be avenged, and human reason offered him every excuse. That he laid down his arms at the bidding of an unarmed prophet is, the commentators feel, the one wise and pious act of his reign — the more so because he 'thought they had good grounds' for the war and yielded all the same. To relinquish a just-seeming cause because God forbids it is a harder submission than to abandon a hopeless one. The verse thus closes the political drama on a note of unexpected reverence: the same king who could not stoop to speak a soft word to his people can, at least this once, bow to the plain word of the Lord. And Clarke's aside about the war-weariness of ordinary men gives the scene a human warmth — the soldiers, glad to be spared, disband and 'return every man to his house,' the kingdom's blood unshed because the God who divided it would not have brethren destroy one another.
25:
The scene shifts to the new king, and the witnesses read Jeroboam's building works as prudent statecraft shadowed by coming apostasy. The grammatical-historical Ellicott lavishes attention on Shechem's long history — a city already standing when Abraham entered the land (Genesis 12:6), a city of refuge and the scene of the Law's solemn recital (Joshua 20:7; 24:1), later the seat of Abimelech's usurpation and destroyed (Judges 9). Jeroboam is said to have 'built it anew,' whether raising it from ruin or simply fortifying it as his capital; and he rebuilt Penuel too (Genesis 32:30-31), commanding the eastern road, 'as an outpost to his new capital.' Keil and Delitzsch concur that 'built' here means 'fortifying,' since both towns had long stood. Gill likewise takes it that Jeroboam 'repaired the walls, fortified it, and built a palace for his residence.'

The puritan Matthew Henry grants the wisdom of the fortifications but fastens on the fatal project they framed. Jeroboam 'built Shechem first and then Penuel,' and 'this might be proper; but he formed another project for the establishing of his kingdom which was fatal to the interests of religion.' Two roots of that project Henry exposes. First, jealous fear: Jeroboam 'could put no confidence in the affections of his people,' for 'what is got by wrong and usurpation cannot be enjoyed nor kept with any security.' Second, and deeper, unbelief: he was 'distrustful of the promise of God,' who had pledged to build him 'a sure house' if he kept close to his duty (1 Kings 11:38), yet he 'would contrive ways and means, and sinful ones too, for his own safety.' Henry's diagnosis becomes a maxim: 'A practical disbelief of God's all-sufficiency is at the bottom of all our treacherous departures from him.'

The witnesses thus set two things side by side without confusing them. The building of the two fortress-cities was legitimate, even sound — a new king securing his frontiers east and west of Jordan. But the same anxiety that raised the walls would, in the next verses, raise the golden calves; the fortifying of the kingdom against Rehoboam would slide into the fortifying of it against God. Ellicott's rich topography reminds the reader that Shechem carried the memory of patriarchs and of covenant renewal, making Jeroboam's choice of it as capital heavy with irony: the city of Joshua's 'choose you this day' would become the seat of a king who chose calves. And Henry's twofold indictment — fear of man and distrust of God — names the spiritual disease that turned a promising reign into the byword 'Jeroboam who made Israel to sin.'
26:
The narrator opens Jeroboam's heart — 'Jeroboam said in his heart, Now shall the kingdom return to the house of David' — and the witnesses read the verse as the birth of a calculated apostasy. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the reasoning exactly. To give his kingdom 'internal strength,' Jeroboam 'resolved to provide for his subjects a substitute for the sacrificial worship in the temple,' so as 'to take away all occasion for making festal journeys to Jerusalem, from which he apprehended, and that probably not without reason, a return of the people to the house of David and consequently further danger for his own life.' His fear was not baseless: the pull of Zion's worship might well draw the tribes back to their old king.

The reformed Gill notes the setting of the soliloquy — Jeroboam 'musing about the state of his kingdom and the affairs of it' — a king turning his political anxieties over in private and reaching, in that solitude, a fateful decision. The phrase 'said in his heart' matters to the witnesses: this is not a policy forced on him by advisers but a scheme conceived in his own breast, the fruit of his own reasoning about how to keep a crown.

The commentators feel the tragic logic of the moment. Everything in Jeroboam's premise is shrewd and everything in his conclusion is ruinous. He sees truly that religion binds a people, that 'if they cleave to their old religion, they will go back to their old king'; his mistake is to conclude that he must therefore change the religion rather than trust the God who gave him the throne. The very insight that should have driven him to Jerusalem drives him instead to devise a rival worship. Keil and Delitzsch's phrase 'probably not without reason' is important: the danger Jeroboam feared was real, which makes his sin the more instructive — he met a genuine threat with an ungodly remedy, preferring the security of contrivance to the security of obedience. God had promised him a sure house on the sole condition of faithfulness (1 Kings 11:38); Jeroboam, weighing that promise against the visible magnetism of the temple, trusted the calculation over the covenant. The verse is thus the hinge on which his reign, and the whole northern kingdom, turns toward idolatry — a decision reached not in a moment of passion but in cold, self-preserving thought, 'in his heart,' where the worst departures from God are so often first conceived.
27:
Jeroboam's fear is stated in full — that if the people go up to sacrifice at Jerusalem their heart will turn to Rehoboam, and 'they shall kill me' — and the witnesses weigh the mixture of shrewd insight and faithless dread that drives it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay the political logic bare and note the practical bind: the people were bound to go up to the house of the Lord three times a year, and Jeroboam judged, not unreasonably, that such regular pilgrimage to David's city would erode their new allegiance. He therefore 'consulted with his counsellors, or the heads of the nation, who had helped him to the throne,' and resolved on the golden calves that the next verse describes.

The reformed Gill spells out the obligation: the people were required to sacrifice in the temple 'three times in the year, besides other times, which they might do nowhere else' — so the tug toward Jerusalem was constant and lawful, woven into the very calendar of Israel's faith. The wesleyan Clarke fastens on the naked self-interest in the words 'they shall kill me': Jeroboam 'found he had little cause to trust this fickle people; though they had declared for him, it was more from caprice, desire of change, and novelty, than from any regular and praiseworthy principle.' The king reads his subjects rightly as unstable, and trusts them not at all.

The witnesses converge on the spiritual failure hidden in a sound analysis. Jeroboam is not wrong about the power of worship to shape loyalty, nor wrong that his throne rested on a shifting popular mood. His fault is that, seeing all this, he never once considers the promise of God as his security. The same divine word that gave him the kingdom had pledged him a sure house on the condition of obedience; had he believed it, he might have sent his people up to Jerusalem in confidence, trusting God to keep the crown God had given. Instead, dread of assassination and loss governs him wholly, and he prefers to bend religion to politics rather than politics to religion. Clarke's shrewd note on the people's caprice cuts both ways: it justifies Jeroboam's distrust of men and condemns his distrust of God, for the very fickleness that makes human allegiance untrustworthy is the reason a king should rest on the unchanging promise instead. The verse shows fear reasoning its way, step by plausible step, toward the great sin — every premise granted, the conclusion still a betrayal, because at no point does the frightened king lift his eyes above the calculations of self-preservation to the God who had raised him up.
28:
The great sin is committed — 'the king made two calves of gold' — and the witnesses concentrate on what the calves were and why Jeroboam chose them. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify the objects: the golden calves were 'young oxen,' probably like Aaron's calf, 'made of a kernel of wood covered with gold plate,' and they insist Jeroboam had in mind 'not merely the Egyptian Apis-worship generally, but more especially the image-worship which Aaron introduced at Sinai' — for he echoes Aaron's very cry. Ellicott sets out the tangle of causes behind the choice. The repetition of Aaron's words (Exodus 32:8) marks it 'a revival of that ancient idolatry'; the animal-worship of Egypt, lately familiar to Jeroboam, suggested the form; the bull was 'the emblem of Ephraim'; and the cherubim and the oxen of the temple furniture may have lent the calf a false sanction. The Psalmist's scorn frames the whole — turning 'the glory of God into the similitude of a calf that eateth hay' (Psalms 106:20).

Crucially, the witnesses agree on the calves' intent. They were meant to represent, or rather to make present, not a false god but the true one — 'Behold thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt.' Ellicott is explicit: 'this idolatry... was a breach, not of the First Commandment, but of the Second' — the forbidden making of 'a similitude' of the true God (Deuteronomy 4:15-18). Yet, he warns, 'like all such veneration of images, it probably degenerated,' sliding from symbol to local presence, and so 'perhaps to a veiled polytheism, certainly to a superstitious and carnal conception of the Godhead.'

The wesleyan Clarke sees the political cynicism plainly: Jeroboam 'invented a political religion,' pretending it would be 'inconvenient and oppressive' for the people to go to Jerusalem — 'not the last time that religion was made a state engine to serve political purposes.' He marvels at the effrontery of using Aaron's exact words 'when they must have heard what terrible judgments fell upon their forefathers for this idolatry.' The reformed Gill notes the counsel taken with men 'that had as little religion as himself, and were only concerned for the civil state.' The witnesses do not divide: the sin lay not in inventing a new deity but in daring to image the true God against his express command, and in bending the worship of Jehovah to the ends of a throne — a corruption that, however it began as symbol, was bound to rot into gross idolatry.
29:
The calves are stationed at the two ends of the land — 'the one in Bethel, and the other in Dan' — and the witnesses read the placement as both political and darkly opportune. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch stress the fundamental breach: the institution 'became a sin' because it violated 'the fundamental law of the Old Testament religion,' which not only forbade all worship of Jehovah under images (Exodus 20:4) but had 'not even left the choice of the place of worship to the people' (Deuteronomy 12:5). Two commandments and the whole principle of the central sanctuary are overthrown at a stroke.

Ellicott shows why these two towns were shrewdly chosen: as 'the frontier towns of the kingdom,' one at each extremity, they made the new worship convenient north and south; but they also carried 'associations of their own.' Bethel preserved 'the memory of Jacob's vision, and of his consecration of the place as a sanctuary' (Genesis 28:19; 35:14-15), and had long been a place of religious assembly. At Dan, the old local sanctuary set up at the Danite conquest of the city still lingered, its priesthood descended from Jonathan the grandson of Moses down 'to the day of the captivity of the land' (Judges 18:30) — so 'these priests of this old worship became naturally the appointed ministers of the new.' Jeroboam thus grafted his innovation onto genuine sacred memory, giving idolatry the look of ancestral piety.

The reformed Gill notes the same double motive for Bethel, chosen 'because its name signifies the house of God, and had been a sacred place, where Jacob more than once enjoyed the divine Presence' — the very holiness of the site lent colour to the corruption. The wesleyan Clarke sets the whole in its longer history: 'Solomon's idolatry had prepared the people for Jeroboam's abominations,' the calves at the southern and northern extremities completing a work of apostasy already begun. The witnesses converge on the cunning of the scheme and the gravity of the sin. Jeroboam did not invent his shrines out of nothing; he seized on Bethel's patriarchal glory and Dan's lingering irregular cult, so that the people might worship the calves without feeling they had abandoned their fathers' faith. That very plausibility was its danger. What looked like reverence for ancient holy places was in truth the overthrow of the law that fixed worship at Jerusalem and forbade all images, and the commentators see in it the pattern of every corruption that clothes disobedience in the borrowed garments of tradition.
30:
The narrator pronounces the verdict — 'and this thing became a sin' — and the witnesses weigh both the sentence and the puzzling clause that follows, 'for the people went to worship before the one, even unto Dan.' The reformed Gill states the moral progression plainly: the calf-worship became 'the cause and occasion of the sin of idolatry; it led them by degrees to leave off the worship of God, and to worship these calves as gods.' What Jeroboam intended as a symbol of Jehovah slid, just as Ellicott had warned, into the thing it merely pictured, until the image itself was adored.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott addresses the textual difficulty in 'even unto Dan.' Some have thought 'words referring to Bethel have fallen out,' but he finds 'no sign of such variation' in the Septuagint or other versions — though the Greek in some manuscripts adds the telling gloss 'and deserted the house of the Lord.' He explains the singling out of Dan by its readiness: 'there the old sanctuary remained, and the priesthood was ready,' so the people could 'go to worship at once,' whereas Bethel still needed the temple and priesthood that the following verses describe. The 'house of high places,' he adds, took its name partly from its position, partly to link it with the condemned 'high places' of the Law. Keil and Delitzsch read the phrase differently but to like effect: 'the people even to Dan' means the people throughout the whole kingdom, from the southern boundary marked by Bethel all the way to the northern town.

The witnesses agree on the substance. Whatever the exact syntax of 'even unto Dan,' the verse records that the calf-cult took hold — that the people embraced it eagerly, some travelling the length of the land to the northern shrine. Gill's earlier observation elsewhere sharpens the irony the commentators feel: those who had complained that it was too far to go up to Jerusalem thought nothing of journeying twice as far to Dan, to worship God 'according to their own inventions.' The little clause thus does more than fix geography; it exposes the heart of false worship, which will spend any labour on what it has chosen for itself while grudging the smallest obedience to what God has appointed. And the flat verdict 'this thing became a sin' hangs over the whole northern kingdom from this point on, the seed of the ruin that its prophets would trace, again and again, back to the calves of Jeroboam.
31:
Jeroboam completes his rival system with a rival priesthood — 'he made priests of the lowest of the people, which were not of the sons of Levi' — and the witnesses read the verse as apostasy hardening into institution. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the priesthood beside the coming feast, noting how the king built 'a house of high places' and then transferred the appointed feast of the seventh month to the eighth (Leviticus 23:34), pleading perhaps that 'in the northern portion of the kingdom the corn ripened a month later than in Judah.' The whole apparatus is a deliberate counterfeit of the Jerusalem order, altered just enough to be Jeroboam's own.

The wesleyan Clarke dwells on the two innovations. The 'house of high places' he takes as 'a temple of temples' — one chief shrine over the many high places, 'a sort of pantheon' where 'different idols were set up.' And the new priests he judges without mercy: Jeroboam 'took the people indifferently as they came, and made them priests, till he had enough, without troubling himself whether they were of the family of Aaron or the house of Levi.' They seem to have been 'worthless, good-for-nothing fellows, who had neither piety nor good sense' — 'any priests would do well enough for such gods.' Yet Clarke adds a hopeful surmise: 'Probably the sons of Levi had grace enough to refuse to sanction this new priesthood and idolatrous worship.'

The reformed Gill reads the same details soberly, taking the 'house of high places' as a temple at Dan with 'several altars in it for sacrifice, both for burnt offerings and for incense, as at Jerusalem.' The witnesses converge on the significance of an unconsecrated priesthood. In casting off the Aaronic line, Jeroboam severed his worship from the one order God had ordained, and staffed his altars with whoever would serve — the surest sign that the cult existed for the king's convenience, not for God's glory. The commentators feel the contempt for holiness in the phrase 'the lowest of the people': the priesthood that God had hedged with the strictest sanctity is thrown open to any comer, because the god being served is a calf of Jeroboam's own devising, and a made god needs only made priests. Clarke's suggestion that the true Levites refused the office adds a gleam of fidelity to the scene, and indeed the record elsewhere tells how priests and Levites forsook the northern kingdom for Judah rather than serve the calves. The verse shows how thoroughly Jeroboam replaced the divine institution with a human one — place, priest, and feast all remade to bind the people to his throne rather than to their God.
32:
Jeroboam institutes his counterfeit feast — 'a feast in the eighth month, on the fifteenth day, like unto the feast that is in Judah' — and the witnesses fasten on the calculated mimicry and the presumption it concealed. The grammatical-historical Ellicott is sharpest. The 'feast that was in Judah' is clearly the Feast of Tabernacles, kept on the fifteenth of the seventh month; Jeroboam's festival 'at once challenged likeness to' it — Tabernacles being the very occasion of Solomon's temple dedication (1 Kings 8:2) — 'and yet took liberty to alter the date,' fixing it in the month 'which he had devised of his own heart.' Thus he assumed 'the right to set aside the letter of the old law, while professing still to observe the worship of Jehovah.' And in the words 'he offered upon the altar' Ellicott hears a further usurpation: Jeroboam taking on himself 'both functions of the priestly office — to offer sacrifice and to burn incense,' perhaps having 'witnessed the exclusive prominence of Solomon' at the dedication 'and desired to imitate and outdo it.'

The wesleyan Clarke reads the strategy the same way: Jeroboam 'would meet the prejudices of the people as far as he could,' appointing a feast so like the true one that he seemed 'to hold the thing while he subverted the ordinance.' The reformed Gill records both the arbitrary motive and a milder possibility — that Abarbinel thought the later month chosen because 'the fruits of the land were not so soon ripe' in the north, and that Jeroboam 'might hope to get more people to come to his feast when all were gathered in.'

The witnesses converge on the essence of the sin, which is will-worship — the substitution of a man's own devising for God's command under the very forms of devotion. Jeroboam keeps the shape of Tabernacles but moves its day; he keeps the language of sacrifice but seizes the priest's office; he keeps the name of Jehovah but changes everything Jehovah appointed. The phrase 'which he had devised of his own heart' is the indictment the commentators return to again and again: true religion receives its worship from God, but false religion invents it, and the mark of the invention is precisely this pious resemblance to the real thing. By copying the feast just closely enough to soothe the people's conscience while altering it just enough to bind them to his own establishment, Jeroboam perfected the deception. The verse thus gathers up the whole apostasy — new place, new priests, new feast, new date, and the king himself at the altar — all wearing the borrowed dress of the worship of Israel's God.
33:
The chapter closes on Jeroboam at his altar — 'so he offered upon the altar which he had made in Bethel the fifteenth day of the eighth month, even in the month which he had devised of his own heart' — and the witnesses read the repeated verse as both summary and threshold. The grammatical-historical Ellicott explains the seeming redundancy: the verse's repetition 'is accounted for by its belonging properly in sense to the next chapter, opening the story of the mission of the man of God from Judah.' Its force, he says, is best caught by rendering the verbs as continuous — 'so Jeroboam was offering' — a picture of the king in the very act of his false worship when the prophet of the following chapter will confront him. Keil and Delitzsch simply set the verse whole, underlining the fatal phrase 'the month which he had devised of his own heart,' and the double record that 'he offered upon the altar, and burnt incense.'

The wesleyan Clarke names the usurpation without softening it: 'Jeroboam probably performed the functions of high priest himself, that he might in his own person condense the civil and ecclesiastical power.' Here is the culmination of the whole scheme — one man gathering into himself throne and altar, king and priest, so that the worship of the nation should depend wholly on him. The reformed Gill notes only that he did at Bethel 'as he had done in Dan,' completing at both shrines the apostasy he had planned.

The witnesses let the verse stand as the finished portrait of a self-made religion. Every clause records a human devising: the altar 'which he had made,' the day and month 'which he had devised of his own heart,' the sacrifice and incense offered by a king who had appointed himself priest. Nothing in it came from God; all of it came from Jeroboam. And the commentators feel the ominous poise of the ending. The imperfect tense that Ellicott presses — the king caught mid-offering — leaves him standing at the altar with his hand outstretched, precisely where the man of God from Judah will find him and cry against the altar in the Lord's name. So the chapter that began with a kingdom rent by one king's pride ends with that kingdom's worship corrupted by another king's fear, and both, the witnesses have insisted throughout, within the overruling purpose of God. Jeroboam's 'own heart' has now wholly replaced the divine appointment; the stage is set for heaven to answer his invented altar with a word he cannot silence.
